channel.title: The Cypherpunk Handbook - Articles
channel.description: Latest articles and updates from the Cypherpunk Handbook.

item.title: Terms and explanations of security in operating systems.
item.description: This document is dedicated to giving a brief but precise explanation of several terms related to security in operating systems, which can confuse even the most experienced people. This document is not intended to explain all the terms used and documented in all operating systems, nor specific programs, but only universal concepts.

item.title: Why OpenBSD stands alone
item.description: This article explains why I consider OpenBSD one of the most secure operating systems in the world, and why I think Windows, macOS, iOS, Android, and Linux remain weaker even when hardened. I also compare it with other BSDs, Qubes OS, Fedora Secureblue, and Hardened Gentoo.

item.title: The C language's biggest problem: evidence of its insecurity
item.description: C is not a memory-safe language. It is possible to write secure software in C, but flawless memory management is not a realistic expectation, no matter how skilled the programmers are or how many reviews the code receives. Formal verification can help in rare cases; the seL4 microkernel and the CompCert compiler are two examples. But those projects are exceptions, not the normal path for most software.

item.title: From a strict security perspective, Qubes OS is not reliable
item.description: This article is a critical look at Qubes OS, or "XenOS" as I sometimes call it. I focus on its security weaknesses, note where its design is useful, and suggest improvements and alternatives that I consider more reliable for different kinds of users.
